Make sure you have install anpan from [the main anpan_tutorial page](https://github.com/biobakery/biobakery/wiki/anpan_tutorial)!

Anpan - for strain epidemiology 

It's difficult to find associations between microbial strains and host health outcomes due to their fine resolution and non-recurrence across individuals. This package, anpan, aims to make inferring those relationships a bit easier by providing an interface to our strain analysis functionality. For this tutorial, we will use data from from a large pooled analysis of colorectal cancer (CRC) metagenomes spanning 18 cohorts ([Piccinno et al., 2025](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40461820/)). Specifically we will focus on the data in Figure 6 of this publication. The tutorial will cover two modules of anpan's functionality:

Modeling the association between outcome variables (here CRC stage) and the phylogeny of strains within a given microbial species - Phylogenetic modeling

Modeling the association between outcome variables (CRC stage) and microbial gene presence while accounting for covariates - Per-species gene models

---
  
  <h2>Table of Contents</h2>
  
  <!-- TOC -->
  
  - [PGLMMs](#pglmms)
    - [Input data](#input-data)
      - [Phylogenetic tree](#phylogenetic-tree)
        - [Metadata](#metadata)
          - [Visualize your tree](#visualize-your-tree)
            - [Fit the PGLMM](#fit-the-pglmm)
              - [Gene model](#gene-model)
                - [Prepare gene family data](#prepare-gene-family-data)
                  - [Run the gene model](#run-the-gene-model)
                    - [Examine filtering diagnostics](#examine-filtering-diagnostics)
                      - [Examine gene model results](#examine-gene-model-results)
                        
                        <!-- /TOC -->
                          
                          ---
                          
## Input data

Here, we are testing whether strain-level variation in *R. bicirculans* — as captured by its within-species phylogenetic and gene carriage patterns — is structured according to CRC disease stage broken into early stage (stages 0-II) and late stage (II-IV) tumors. 

The first step will be launching R. You can also complete this tutorial locally in R/RStudio. 

```bash
# Launch R
R
```

### Phylogenetic (PGLMM) Models
Phylogenetic generalized linear mixed models (PGLMMs) are probabilistic models that account for phylogenetic structure.

For a PGLMM with a linear regression as the base model, the model structure is as follows:
  
  $$ y = X \beta + (1|\text{leaf}) + \epsilon $$
  
  $$(1|\text{leaf}) \sim \text{MVNormal}(0, \sigma_p^2\Omega)$$
  
  $$\epsilon \sim \text{Normal}(0, \sigma_R^2)$$
  
The outcome $y$ is modeled as a function of some familiar terms: covariates $X$, coefficients $\beta$, and residual noise $\epsilon$. The key addition is the phylogenetic term $(1|\text{leaf})$, which contains a "random effect" for each observation in $y$. Unlike typical random effects (which are usually independent between different levels of the random effect variable), the values in the phylogenetic term follow a pre-specified correlation structure $\Omega$, which is derived from a tree. The variability of the phylogenetic term is scaled by the "phylogenetic noise" parameter $\sigma_{phylo}$.

To understand how a tree implies a correlation structure, consider the plot below showing a phylogenetic tree and the lower triangle of the correlation matrix it implies. You can see that the clade on the left (with low inter-leaf distances between its members) forms a bright sub-block of high correlation in the matrix. The tiny clade on the far right has high inter-leaf distance between its members and the rest of the tree, so its members generally have low correlation with the rest of the leaves, exhibited by the dark band on the right of the triangle. Note that the matrix is derived solely from the structure of the tree (the black lines) -- it is not influenced by the colored outcome dots. The correlation matrix numerically quantifies the "higher inter-leaf distance → lower correlation" principle.

<img src="https://github.com/biobakery/anpan/blob/main/vignettes/vignette_figs/tree_hcm.png?raw=true" width="60%"/>
  
In this tutorial we will use a real-world example: a StrainPhlAn tree built from *Roseburia bicirculans* (SGB4262), derived from a large pooled analysis of colorectal cancer (CRC) metagenomes spanning 18 cohorts ([Piccinno et al., 2025](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40461820/)). We will use the PGLMM to test whether strain-level phylogenetic structure in *R. bicirculans* is associated with early vs. late stage CRC. After loading the data, we'll fit the PGLMM with `anpan_pglmm()` and examine the results. We will then follow up by running the gene model to identify which specific genes in *R. bicirculans* are most associated with CRC stage.


For this tutorial, we will be using a StrainPhlAn 4 tree built from SGB4262, which corresponds to the species *Ruminococcus bicirculans*. StrainPhlAn is a tool for reconstructing the dominant clade of a species within a sample. Briefly, StrainPhlAn takes all the sequences that mapped to the markers for a species in [MetaPhlAn](https://github.com/biobakery/biobakery/wiki/metaphlan4) and assesses the reads for well-covered, consistent single nucleotide level substitutions compared to the reference markers. It then builds a phylogenetic tree comparing the dominant species-level clade across all samples in the dataset, producing a within-species phylogenetic tree. See the [StrainPhlAn 4 tutorial](https://github.com/biobakery/biobakery/wiki/strainphlan4) for details on how to generate this tree from your own data.

*R. bicirculans* is a member of the *Ruminococcaceae* family and has been identified as a species of interest in CRC research. 

Read the tree into R using the `ape` library:

```{R}
# Load the needed libraries
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(tibble)
library(dplyr)
library(ape)
library(anpan)

# Read in the StrainPhlAn tree for R. bicirculans (SGB4262)
tree = read.tree("./input/RAxML_bestTree.t__SGB4262.StrainPhlAn4.tre")
```

### Metadata

The samples used to build this tree come from a pooled CRC metagenomics dataset spanning multiple studies and countries. The metadata file contains sample identifiers, the source study, and CRC staging information. If you are running this tutorial as part of a biobakery course the metadata is in ./input/metadata, if you are not, you can find the metadata via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15069069. 

Read in the metadata and inspect it:

```{R}
meta = fread("./input/metadata.tsv")

head(meta)
```

For the PGLMM we need a binary outcome variable. Here, we will classify samples as either **early stage** (stages 0-II) or **late stage** (stages III-IV) CRC. This distinction is clinically meaningful: early-stage CRC is typically localized and has a better prognosis, while late-stage CRC has spread regionally or distantly. We are testing whether strain-level phylogenetic structure in *R. bicirculans* differs between these two groups.

```{R}
# Create binary outcome: early (stages 0-II) vs. late (stages III-IV) stage CRC
# Adjust the column name and stage labels below to match your metadata exactly
meta <- meta %>%
  filter(`Tumor Staging AJCC` %in% c("0", "I", "II", "III", "IV")) %>%
  mutate(stage_binary = factor(
    ifelse(`Tumor Staging AJCC` %in% c("0", "I", "II"), "early", "late"),
    levels = c("early", "late")
  ))

table(meta$stage_binary)

#to fit this into a logical - let's make early == FALSE and late == TRUE
meta <- meta %>% mutate(stage_binary = stage_binary == "late")

# Rename sample ID column to match anpan's expected format
# Adjust the column name on the left to match your metadata exactly
names(meta)[names(meta) == "Name"] = "sample_id"
                        
# Check that your tree tip labels are present in the metadata
intersect(meta$sample_id, tree$tip.label)
```
                        
A few things to verify at this point:
 - The `sample_id` column must **exactly match** the tip labels in the tree. If your tip labels have a suffix like `_bowtie2` (which some of ours do), you can use the `trim_pattern` argument in `anpan_pglmm()` to strip it.
- The outcome variable must be a **factor with exactly two levels**.
- Samples in the tree that are reference genomes (not from the CRC cohort) should be removed from the metadata so they are excluded from the model. These will automatically be dropped since they won't match any `sample_id` in the metadata.

## Visualize your tree

Before fitting the model, it is useful to visualize the tree with the outcome variable overlaid. You can do this with `plot_outcome_tree()`:

```{R}
plot_outcome_tree(tree, meta, covariates = NULL, outcome = "stage_binary")
```

This will show the StrainPhlAn tree with tips colored by CRC stage (early vs. late).

**Question:** Does it look like CRC stage is associated with phylogenetic structure in *R. bicirculans*? Do early and late stage samples tend to cluster together on the tree, or do they appear interleaved? How confident are you in that visual assessment, and how might it affect your interpretation of the model results?

## Fit the PGLMM

The code chunk below shows how to use `anpan_pglmm()` to fit a PGLMM testing whether strain-level phylogenetic structure in *R. bicirculans* is associated with CRC stage. This will take a couple of minutes to run.

A few things to note about the arguments:

- We set `family = "binomial"` because our outcome is binary (early vs. late stage).
- We set `reg_noise = FALSE` as required for binomial models.
- `loo_comparison = TRUE` runs a leave-one-out model comparison against a base model without the phylogenetic component. A positive LOO difference indicates that the phylogenetic term improves model fit.
- We set `run_diagnostics = FALSE` to skip Stan sampler diagnostics for the purposes of this tutorial, but it is good practice to inspect these in real analyses.

```{R}

meta_path = "~/Desktop/anpan_tutorial/metadata_cleaned.tsv"
fwrite(meta, file = meta_path, sep = "\t")

tree_path = "~/Desktop/anpan_tutorial/RAxML_bestTree.t__SGB4262.StrainPhlAn4.tre"

results_anpan = anpan_pglmm(meta_path,
                             tree_path,
                             out_dir         = "./output",
                             outcome         = "stage_binary",
                             family          = "binomial",
                             bug_name        = "R.bicirculans",
                             reg_noise       = FALSE,
                             loo_comparison  = TRUE,
                             run_diagnostics = FALSE,
                             refresh         = 500,
                             show_plot_tree  = TRUE,
                             show_post       = TRUE,
                             trim_pattern    = "_bowtie2")
```

Once the model finishes, examine the LOO comparison result to assess whether the phylogenetic term meaningfully improves the model:

```{R}
results_anpan$loo
```

A positive ELPD difference (with magnitude greater than roughly 2-4 times its standard error) suggests that the phylogenetic structure in *R. bicirculans* is informative for predicting CRC stage. So here having a base fit of ~-14, improves substainally in the pglmm mode (0) - thus in this case we can conclude that the structuring of this tree is related to the outcome. However, a resulting difference near zero suggests that strain-level variation in this species does not meaningfully track with disease stage in this dataset, even if the tree shows some visual clustering.

By default, `anpan_pglmm()` will also produce a correlation matrix heatmap derived from the tree, showing which samples share phylogenetic signal. Setting `show_plot_tree = TRUE` and `show_post = TRUE` will produce a tree visualization with the estimated posterior phylogenetic effects overlaid on the tips, allowing you to see which strains are estimated to be associated with early vs. late stage CRC.

You can also generate this visualization explicitly after the model run using `plot_tree_with_post()`:

```{R}
plot_tree_with_post(tree_file  = tree,
                    meta_file  = results_anpan$model_input,
                    fit        = results_anpan$pglmm_fit,
                    covariates = NULL,
                    outcome    = "stage_binary",
                    color_bars = FALSE,
                    ladderize  = TRUE,
                    omit_na    = TRUE,
                    labels     = levels(results_anpan$model_input$sample_id), 
                    trim_pattern    = "_bowtie2"
                    )
```

Note: for `labels` and `meta_file` it is more correct to use the anpan result object rather than the original metadata file, to ensure correct mapping of the data to the tree tips.

See the [tutorial with synthetic data](https://github.com/biobakery/biobakery/wiki/anpan_pglmm) for a more thorough explanation of the model diagnostics and output interpretation.

We could also add some covariates to this model to see how they impact the outcome. For instance in this case we have data for country of sample collection, age of the individual, BMI, and tumor location to name a few. If there is time, retry the modeling adding in the covariate of country and see how that changes the model. 

---

# Gene model

The PGLMM above told us whether strain-level phylogenetic structure in *R. bicirculans* is associated with CRC stage - in other words, whether some strains of this species tend to be more common in early vs. late stage disease. But it doesn't tell us *why*. To follow up, we can use the anpan gene model to ask a more specific question: **which genes in *R. bicirculans* are differentially abundant between early and late stage CRC?**
                          
The gene model uses species-stratified functional profiles from [HUMAnN](https://huttenhower.sph.harvard.edu/humann) to test each gene for association with the outcome, while filtering out samples where the species is too poorly covered to contribute reliable signal. For a full description of the gene model methodology, see the [gene model tutorial](https://github.com/biobakery/biobakery/wiki/anpan_gene_model).
                        
## Prepare gene family data
                        
The gene model requires a per-species gene family abundance file produced by HUMAnN. If you have already run HUMAnN on your samples and split the output by species (see the [gene model tutorial](https://github.com/biobakery/biobakery/wiki/anpan_gene_model) for those steps), you should have a file named something like:
                          
```
g__Ruminococcus.s__Ruminococcus_bicirculans.tsv
```
For the tutorial - this file can be found in the input directory

                        
Take a quick look at the gene file to confirm the format is correct - genes should be in the first column formatted as `gene_id|taxon_id`, with one column per sample:
                          
```{R}
genes = fread("./input/g__Ruminococcus.s__Ruminococcus_bicirculans.tsv") %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  column_to_rownames(var = names(.)[1])

head(genes)
dim(genes)

```

                        
The `sample_id` column in the metadata must exactly match the column names in the gene file. We are reusing the same metadata file we prepared for the PGLMM above, so the `sample_id` column and the `stage_binary` outcome column should already be in place.
                        
## Run the gene model
                        
We can now run the gene model with `anpan()`. We use the same binary outcome (`stage_binary`) and the same metadata file as the PGLMM, so the two analyses are directly comparable. We set `model_type = "fastglm"` for efficient frequentist logistic regression with FDR correction across genes.
                        
```{R}

gene_path = "~/Desktop/anpan_tutorial/g__Ruminococcus.s__Ruminococcus_bicirculans.tsv"

out_dir = "./output"
getwd()
anpan_res = anpan(bug_file          = gene_path,
                  meta_file         = meta_path,
                  out_dir           = out_dir,
                  covariates        = NULL,
                  outcome           = "stage_binary",
                  model_type        = "fastglm",
                  discretize_inputs = TRUE)
```
                        
                        
Let's look at the top results:

```{R}
head(anpan_res)
```

`anpan()` returns a data.table with regression statistics for the effect of each gene on CRC stage. The `estimate` column gives the estimate (effect size) for late vs. early stage, `q_bug_wise` is the FDR-corrected q-value within this species, and `bug_name` confirms we are looking at *R. bicirculans*. Genes with a low q-value and a large absolute estimate are your candidate hits.

## Examine filtering diagnostics

Before interpreting the results it is important to check that the filtering step performed sensibly. The diagnostic plots are written to `output/filter_stats/plots/`. There are two plots to examine for *R. bicirculans*:

**K-means dot plot:** Each dot is a sample. The x-axis is the number of non-zero gene entries in that sample and the y-axis is the median log abundance of those non-zero entries. The k-means clustering colors dots by the inferred presence/absence of *R. bicirculans*. You want to see two reasonably well-separated clusters - one group of samples with many non-zero genes at high abundance (bug present) and one with few non-zero genes at low abundance (bug absent or very low coverage).

**Gene profile lines plot:** Each line is a sample, showing the log-abundance of genes ranked from highest to lowest. Well-covered samples (blue) should show a clear plateau at moderate-to-high abundance before dropping off, while poorly covered samples (red) should drop off quickly. Good separation between the red and blue lines gives confidence that the filtering is working correctly.

If the two groups are not clearly separated in either plot, consider whether manual curation of the sample set is appropriate before re-running with `filtering_method = "none"`.

## Examine gene model results

We can visualize the gene model results with `plot_results()`. We first read in the filtered model input from the `filter_stats` directory, then pass it along with the model results:

```{R}
input_path = file.path(out_dir, "filter_stats",
                       "filtered_Ruminococcus_bicirculans.tsv.gz")

model_input = fread(input_path)

plot_results(res         = anpan_res,
             model_input = model_input,
             covariates  = NULL,
             outcome     = "stage_binary",
             bug_name    = "Ruminococcus_bicirculans",
             cluster     = "both",
             q_threshold = 0.2,
             show_trees  = TRUE)
```

Things to know about the results plot:

- The central tile plot shows the filtered gene abundance matrix - which genes (rows) are present in which samples (columns). The annotation bar at the top shows CRC stage (early vs. late).
- The estimate intervals on the left show the estimates +/- 1.96 x standard error, with nominal significance stars. Positive estimates indicate higher abundance in late-stage CRC relative to early-stage.
- The genes are sorted by significance by default. Setting `cluster = "both"` also clusters genes and samples hierarchically, which can reveal whether the top hits tend to co-occur in the same samples or belong to related pathways.
- The `q_threshold` argument controls which genes are shown. Here we use `0.2` to show genes passing a 20% FDR threshold, but you can tighten this (e.g. `0.05`) or show all genes by setting `q_threshold = 0`.

**Interpreting the results in context:** The PGLMM told us that strain-level phylogenetic structure in *R. bicirculans* carries some signal for CRC stage. The gene model now lets us ask whether that signal is attributable to specific functional differences between strains. If the top gene hits are enriched in particular pathways (e.g. carbohydrate metabolism - from the paper), that provides a functional hypothesis for why certain strains of *R. bicirculans* may be more prevalent in early vs. late stage disease. The full gene-level results (including all model terms) are written to `output/*all_terms.tsv.gz`, and the gene-specific results are in `output/*gene_terms.tsv.gz` for downstream pathway analysis.
